# What Is Generative AI?

Generative artificial intelligence is a class of modeling methods that learn
regularities in data and use them to synthesize new artifacts. In language and
business systems, these artifacts may be summaries, code, structured records,
classifications with rationales, images, forecasts with explanations, or
tool-mediated actions. The technical lineage runs through probabilistic
language modeling, neural representation learning and transformer-based
sequence modeling [@Bengio2003NeuralLM; @Vaswani2017Attention;
@JurafskyMartin2026SLP].

More formally, a generative model represents or approximates a probability
distribution over data, often written as \(p_\theta(x)\), or a conditional
distribution over outputs given inputs and context, written as
\(p_\theta(y \mid x, c)\). Here \(x\) may be source data, \(y\) is the generated
artifact, \(c\) is context such as instructions or retrieved evidence, and
\(\theta\) denotes learned model parameters.

::: {.key-box}
**Key idea.** Generative AI is not simply "AI that writes text." It is
probabilistic artifact synthesis: a model uses learned structure and current
context to produce plausible new data.
:::

::: {#cell-fig-generative-ai-contrast .cell fig-height='2.45' fig-width='8.2' execution_count=1}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Three views of generative AI. (a) Discriminative modeling estimates a boundary or score for labels. (b) Generative modeling learns the structure of the data distribution and can sample new observations. (c) Conditional generation produces artifacts under instructions, evidence and constraints.](what-is-generative-ai_files/figure-html/fig-generative-ai-contrast-output-1.png){#fig-generative-ai-contrast width=1141 height=336}
:::
:::


**Figure 1a** shows the familiar discriminative setting. The model receives
features and learns a decision rule, score, or boundary. Many classical
business models live here: churn prediction, fraud scoring, lead qualification,
credit risk, and response prediction. The output space is fixed in advance.

**Figure 1b** shows the generative setting. Rather than learning only a
boundary, the model learns structure in the data distribution. Once such a
distribution has been learned, the model can sample new observations, fill in
missing information, denoise corrupted inputs, or continue a pattern.

**Figure 1c** shows the modern business GenAI setting. In practice, the model is
only one component. The generated artifact is conditioned by instructions,
evidence, constraints, retrieval, tools, user interface design, and evaluation.
For applied NLP systems, the surrounding design often determines whether the
output is reliable.

# Generative and Discriminative Questions

Discriminative modeling asks questions such as:

$$
\begin{align}
P(y \mid x)
\end{align}
$$

What is the probability of a label \(y\) given an observation \(x\)? A model
might estimate the probability that a customer will churn given recent product
usage.

Generative modeling asks questions such as:

$$
\begin{align}
P(x) \quad \text{or} \quad P(x, y)
\end{align}
$$

What data are likely? How are observations distributed? What new sample would
look like it came from the same process?

Modern large language models often operate conditionally:

$$
\begin{align}
P_\theta(y_1, \ldots, y_T \mid x, c)
\end{align}
$$

The generated text \(y_1, \ldots, y_T\) depends on an input \(x\), context
\(c\), and learned parameters \(\theta\). This is why prompt design, retrieval
quality, and context selection are not cosmetic details. They change the
conditional distribution from which the answer is generated.

# Levels of Generation

"Generative" can refer to several different levels of modeling. At the lowest
level, a model may generate the next symbol in a sequence. At the application
level, the system may generate a structured answer, plan, code block or
document. These levels are related but not identical. A language model may only
be trained to predict tokens, yet an application can use that token-level
behavior to produce a contract summary, a database query or a customer-response
draft.

| Level | Object generated | Typical constraint |
|---|---|---|
| token | next subword or symbol | probability distribution over vocabulary |
| sequence | sentence, paragraph, code block | context and decoding rule |
| structured artifact | JSON, table, citation-bearing answer | schema and validation |
| workflow output | decision memo, ticket update, tool action | business rule and audit trail |

This distinction helps avoid a common misunderstanding. The model generates a
sequence; the **system** turns the sequence into an artifact with meaning,
provenance and consequences. Much of applied GenAI is therefore not new model
training, but careful design of context, constraints and evaluation around a
pretrained model.

# Families of Generative Models

| Family | Learned object | Generation mechanism | Typical artifacts |
|---|---|---|---|
| Autoregressive models | Next-token or next-step distribution | Sequential sampling | Text, code, sequences |
| Diffusion models | Denoising process | Iterative refinement from noise | Images, audio, video |
| Variational autoencoders | Latent representation and decoder | Sample latent variables, decode | Synthetic data, representations |
| Generative adversarial networks | Generator that fools a discriminator | Adversarial optimization | Images, simulation data |
| Retrieval-augmented LLM systems | Conditional generation plus external evidence | Retrieve, condition, generate | Grounded answers and reports |

This taxonomy matters because "generative AI" names a modeling family, not a
single product. A chatbot, image generator, code assistant, synthetic-data
engine, and document-review workflow may all be generative, but the evaluation
criteria differ.

Autoregressive language models are the main focus of these primers because they
sit at the center of modern NLP systems. They generate one token at a time and
therefore make generation a sequence of conditional decisions. Diffusion models
and GANs are important generative families, but they use different objectives
and generation procedures. The shared idea is distribution learning; the
mechanism of sampling differs.

# Business Use Without Losing the NLP Core

For business use, a generative model is useful when it converts language,
records and context into an artifact that supports a decision. The important
point is still linguistic and computational: the system must condition the
model on the right evidence and constrain the artifact to the right form.

| NLP operation | Business-facing artifact | Technical pressure point |
|---|---|---|
| summarization | issue brief or account summary | faithfulness to source text |
| information extraction | structured fields from contracts or forms | schema and boundary decisions |
| semantic comparison | policy or document differences | paraphrase and contradiction |
| code generation | SQL or Python draft | executable correctness |
| grounded generation | answer with evidence | retrieval and citation quality |

The artifact should be judged by the task it supports. A support-ticket summary
must be faithful to tickets. A contract extraction must be complete and
traceable. A generated SQL query must run and answer the intended question. A
strategy memo must separate evidence from speculation.

::: {.pitfall-box}
**Pitfall.** The model objective is not the same as the business objective.
Text that is likely under the model can be unsupported, incomplete, or
misaligned with the decision being made.
:::

# Technical Failure Modes

Generative systems fail in recognizable ways:

- **Unsupported continuation:** the model produces plausible content when
  evidence is absent.
- **Context misuse:** the model ignores, misreads, or overweights supplied
  information.
- **Distribution mismatch:** the deployment task differs from the model's
  training or evaluation setting.
- **Evaluation leakage:** examples or answers used for testing are also present
  in the development process.
- **Interface overtrust:** users treat fluent language as if it were verified
  analysis.

These failures are not mysterious. They follow from using probabilistic models
inside open-ended tasks. The remedy is not simply "better prompting"; it is
better system design: grounding, constraints, tests, monitoring, and human
review.

# Demonstration: From Model Output to System Output

The same generated sentence can have different status depending on the system
around it.

| Generated sentence | System status | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| "The renewal delay was caused by security review." | unsupported continuation | no evidence supplied |
| same sentence with cited notes | grounded claim | evidence can be inspected |
| same sentence in JSON with `cause`, `source`, `confidence` | structured artifact | downstream system can validate fields |
| same sentence after human approval | governed output | accountability is assigned |

The NLP object is a string. The business object is a claim, record or action.
The bridge between the two is evidence and validation.

# Demonstration: Conditional Artifacts

A compact way to analyze any generated artifact is to name its conditioning
variables:

$$
\begin{align}
\text{artifact}
  &= f_\theta(\text{input}, \text{instruction}, \text{evidence},
       \text{constraints}) \\
  &\approx y \sim P_\theta(y \mid x, c).
\end{align}
$$

If the evidence term is empty, the model leans more heavily on learned priors.
If the constraint term is weak, the model may produce the right topic in the
wrong form. If the input is ambiguous, the model may resolve the ambiguity in a
plausible but unverified direction.

::: {.takeaway-box}
**Takeaway.** Generative AI should be understood as distribution learning plus
conditional artifact generation. The model creates possibilities; the analytic
system must make them useful, grounded, and governed.
:::

### References

Classic starting points include neural probabilistic language models
[@Bengio2003NeuralLM], transformers [@Vaswani2017Attention], broad foundation
model framing [@bommasani2022opportunities], and the NLP overview in Jurafsky
and Martin [@JurafskyMartin2026SLP].
